title: Debt Validation Demand
when_to_use:
• A debt collector (not the original creditor) has contacted you about an alleged debt.
• You received the collector's initial written notice (the so-called "Mini-Miranda" or G-Notice) within the last 30 days.
• You want to legally compel the collector to prove (1) that the debt actually exists, (2) that the dollar amount is correct, and (3) that THIS specific agency has the legal right to collect it from you.
• You suspect the collector is a junk-debt buyer who purchased a spreadsheet of accounts and lacks original documents.
• Account details on the collector's notice do not match your records (wrong original creditor, wrong balance, wrong account number, wrong consumer).
• You are pairing this letter with a parallel Stage 1 bureau dispute (Letter 13) so the collector is forced to validate to YOU and verify to the BUREAUS at the same time.
• ALWAYS use this letter FIRST in Stage 2 for any collection account, even if you intend to pay or settle later. Validation gives you leverage; paying without validating gives it away.
why_it_works:
• It triggers the collector's affirmative validation duty under FDCPA §1692g(b) (15 U.S.C. § 1692g(b)). Once you dispute in writing within 30 days of the collector's initial communication, the collector "shall cease collection of the debt, or any disputed portion thereof, until the debt collector obtains verification of the debt" and mails that verification to you. Failure to cease is a strict-liability violation.
• The right to validation is statutory, not contractual. The collector cannot waive it, and your duty to pay is suspended until they comply. Federal courts treat continued collection during the validation window as an automatic FDCPA violation.
• Federal courts and the CFPB have held that credit-report furnishing is a "collection activity" within the meaning of §1692g(b). That means the collector cannot keep reporting the tradeline to Equifax, Experian, or TransUnion until they validate. If they do, you have grounds for both an FDCPA claim under 15 U.S.C. § 1692k AND an FCRA furnisher-accuracy claim under 15 U.S.C. § 1681s-2.
• Junk-debt buyers (Midland, Portfolio Recovery, Jefferson Capital, LVNV/Resurgent, Cavalry, Encore, etc.) routinely cannot produce the four core validation documents: (1) the original signed contract, (2) a complete itemized accounting from a $0 starting balance, (3) the chain of title from the original creditor through every successor in interest, and (4) proof that the collector is licensed in your state. When they cannot produce these, the debt becomes practically uncollectible.
• Statutory damages under FDCPA §1692k (15 U.S.C. § 1692k) are up to $1,000 per case plus actual damages plus reasonable attorney's fees and costs — a fee-shifting structure that makes consumer-protection lawyers willing to take these cases on contingency.
• Validation pressure causes deletion. CFPB enforcement data shows roughly half of disputed collection items are removed when consumers actually invoke §1692g properly — because validating an old account is more expensive than walking away from it.
• It locks the legal posture in place from day one. Even if you later decide to pay, you negotiate from a position where the collector knows you understand the statute and will sue if they violate it.
how_to_use:
STEP 1: Confirm the entity contacting you is a "debt collector" as defined in FDCPA §1692a(6) (15 U.S.C. § 1692a(6)) — generally a third party trying to collect a debt that originated with someone else, or a debt buyer. The original creditor collecting its own current debt is generally NOT covered by the FDCPA.
STEP 2: Calendar the 30-day clock. The validation right runs for 30 days from your receipt of the collector's initial written notice (their G-Notice). Send this letter inside that 30-day window — postmarked, certified-mail tracking should show it. If the 30 days has passed you can still send a written dispute, but the strict §1692g(b) cease-collection lever is strongest inside the 30-day window.
STEP 3: Fill in every [BRACKETED] field carefully. Match the collector's name and address EXACTLY as it appears on their notice (corporate naming variants matter for service of process later). Reference the collector's reference/account number from their notice.
STEP 4: Do NOT include a phone number you want them to use. Force all contact into writing. Some users intentionally omit their phone number on this letter for that reason.
STEP 5: Print on plain white paper. Sign in blue or black ink. Make at least two copies — one for your file, one for your evidence binder. Date the letter the same day you mail it.
STEP 6: Send via USPS Certified Mail with Return Receipt Requested (the green card). Save the receipt and the green card when it returns. The certified-mail tracking number is your evidentiary anchor for everything that follows; without it you cannot prove timely §1692g(b) invocation.
STEP 7: While you wait, do NOT pay anything, do NOT acknowledge the debt by phone, and do NOT enter into any payment arrangement. Any of those acts can waive your validation defenses or, in some states, restart the statute of limitations.
STEP 8: Wait 30 days from the collector's signed receipt date. Three outcomes are possible. (a) The collector ignores you — go to Letter 79 (Demand Removal After Validation Failure) and Letter 80 (Notice of Continued Collection in Violation of §1692g). (b) The collector sends a half-baked computer printout that doesn't actually validate — go to Letter 50 (Validation Follow-Up - Inadequate Response). (c) The collector sends real validation documents — at that point you can decide whether to dispute the underlying merits, settle (Letter 64/65), or pursue Pay-for-Delete (Letter 58).
STEP 9: If at any point during the validation window the collector contacts you again — by phone, by letter, by text, or by reporting to the credit bureaus — that is a §1692g(b) violation. Document it and escalate to Letter 80 and ultimately Letter 86 (FDCPA Improper Collection Practices Demand).

MAILING:
Send certified mail with return receipt requested to the collector's address EXACTLY as printed on their initial notice. Do not send to a different branch, regional office, or PO box than the one they listed. Keep the certified-mail receipt and the returned green card in your evidence binder permanently. The signed green card is the date that triggers the 30-day cease-collection clock under 15 U.S.C. § 1692g(b).

TIMING:
Send within 30 days of your receipt of the collector's initial written communication (their G-Notice). Inside the 30-day window, the §1692g(b) cease-collection lever is automatic. Outside the 30-day window, you can still demand validation and dispute the debt, but the strict cease-collection mandate weakens. Send sooner rather than later — within 7-10 days of receiving the collector's notice is ideal.
template_body:
[Your Full Legal Name]
[Your Address]
[City, State ZIP]
[Your Email Address]

[Date]

[Collector Name]
Attn: Compliance Department
[Collector Address]
[City, State ZIP]

VIA U.S. CERTIFIED MAIL, RETURN RECEIPT REQUESTED
Tracking Number: [Certified Mail Tracking Number]

Re:  FORMAL DEMAND FOR DEBT VALIDATION
      Collector Account / Reference Number: [Collector's Reference Number]
      Original Creditor: [Original Creditor]
      Original Account Number: [Original Account Number - last 4 digits]
      Alleged Balance: [Amount]

Dear [Collector Name]:

I am in receipt of your initial written communication dated [Date of Collector's Initial Notice] regarding the alleged debt referenced above. This letter is my timely written notification, made within the thirty (30) day validation window provided by Section 809 of the Fair Debt Collection Practices Act, 15 U.S.C. § 1692g, that I dispute the validity of this alleged debt in its entirety and demand full validation.

I do not acknowledge this alleged debt. I do not waive any rights, defenses, or claims I may have. I make no admission of liability by sending this letter, and nothing in this correspondence should be construed as such an admission.

Pursuant to 15 U.S.C. § 1692g(b), you must now CEASE all collection activity regarding this alleged debt — including any further written or oral communications with me, any reporting or continued reporting of this account to any consumer reporting agency, any sale or transfer of this account, and any legal action — until you have obtained and mailed to me the verification documents specified below. Federal courts, the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau, and the Federal Trade Commission have repeatedly held that furnishing data to a consumer reporting agency constitutes a "collection activity" within the meaning of § 1692g(b). Continued reporting of this tradeline absent valid verification is therefore a strict-liability violation of the FDCPA.

I.  DOCUMENTS YOU ARE REQUIRED TO PRODUCE

To validate this alleged debt, you must provide me with the following:

1.  Proof that you, [Collector Name], have the legal right to collect this specific debt from me. This means a complete and unbroken chain of title — including every assignment, bill of sale, and forward-flow agreement — from the original creditor, [Original Creditor], to your agency, with my specific account identified on each transfer document. A general portfolio purchase agreement that does not reference my account by name is insufficient.

2.  A copy of the original signed contract, application, or agreement between me and the original creditor that gave rise to this alleged debt. A generic terms-and-conditions document is not sufficient; I am demanding the executed instrument that purports to bind me to this obligation.

3.  A complete, itemized accounting of the alleged debt from a $0.00 opening balance to the present. The accounting must separately identify (a) every charge or transaction that contributed to the principal, (b) every payment or credit applied, (c) every interest charge with the underlying rate and the contractual or statutory authority for that rate, and (d) every fee, with the specific contractual provision or statute that authorizes it. Pursuant to 15 U.S.C. § 1692f(1), you may not collect any amount that is not expressly authorized by the agreement creating the debt or permitted by law.

4.  Documentary proof that the original creditor has, in fact, charged off this account and that the dollar amount you are now demanding is the actual balance owed at the moment your agency acquired the account, plus only such interest and fees as you can show are expressly authorized.

5.  Proof that your agency is licensed and bonded as a debt collector in the State of [Your State of Residence], to the extent required by the laws of that state. If your agency is not licensed where I reside and licensure is required, your collection activity is unlawful and must cease for that reason as well.

6.  Confirmation in writing that this alleged debt is within the applicable statute of limitations for legal action in the State of [Your State of Residence]. If the debt is time-barred, any threat of suit or actual suit on it would itself be a violation of 15 U.S.C. § 1692e(5) and CFPB Regulation F, 12 C.F.R. § 1006.26.

II.  WRITTEN COMMUNICATION ONLY

All further communication regarding this alleged debt must be in writing, mailed to the address shown above. Telephone calls to me at any number — home, mobile, or workplace — are inconvenient and unauthorized, and any such calls after your receipt of this letter will be treated as an independent violation of 15 U.S.C. § 1692c. You are also instructed not to contact any third party (employer, family member, neighbor, or otherwise) regarding this alleged debt; any such contact will be treated as a violation of 15 U.S.C. § 1692c(b).

III.  PRESERVATION OF EVIDENCE

I am preserving all communications, voicemails, account notes, and call records relating to this matter. I expect your agency to do the same. Any destruction or alteration of records relating to this alleged debt after your receipt of this letter will be treated as spoliation of evidence and addressed accordingly in any subsequent litigation.

IV.  CONSEQUENCES OF NONCOMPLIANCE

If you continue to collect this alleged debt — whether by phone, mail, electronic communication, credit-bureau furnishing, sale or transfer to another agency, or legal action — without first providing the validation documents demanded above, I will treat each such act as a separate willful violation of the FDCPA. The Fair Debt Collection Practices Act provides for actual damages, statutory damages of up to $1,000.00 per case, and the recovery of reasonable attorney's fees and costs under 15 U.S.C. § 1692k. I will additionally report your conduct to the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau, the Federal Trade Commission, and the Office of the Attorney General for the State of [Your State of Residence], and I will pursue parallel claims under the Fair Credit Reporting Act, 15 U.S.C. § 1681s-2, for any inaccurate or unverified furnishing.

Govern your conduct accordingly.

Sincerely,

[Your Signature]
[Your Printed Name]

Enclosures (for reference only; original documents retained):
- Copy of your initial collection notice dated [Date of Collector's Initial Notice]
- Copy of envelope showing date of receipt
- Copy of certified-mail receipt for this correspondence
